title: Sweet and Fluffy Lemon Dreams
story: Bakers across the country whip up something magical on March 29 for National Lemon Chiffon Cake Day! This isn't your ordinary cake - it's incredibly light and airy, almost like eating a sweet lemon cloud. The secret is folding beaten egg whites into the batter, making it super fluffy. People celebrate by baking their own chiffon cakes or buying slices from bakeries. Many share photos of their towering, golden creations online, and some even host lemon-themed parties where everything tastes citrusy and bright!
activity_question: What makes lemon chiffon cake so light and fluffy?
activity_choices: Adding beaten egg whites to the batter
Baking it at a very high temperature
Adding fizzy soda to the mix
activity_answer: Adding beaten egg whites to the batter
bonus_fact: Chiffon cake was invented in 1927 but kept secret for 20 years!

title: Cricket Clash: Delhi's Ranji Trophy Triumph

title: American Horse Conquers Dubai's Desert
story: Animal Kingdom galloped into history on March 30th, 2013, when this amazing American racehorse thundered across the finish line first at the Dubai World Cup! This wasn't just any horse race – it was one of the richest and most exciting races in the world, held in the middle of the desert in Dubai. Animal Kingdom had to beat the fastest horses from around the globe, and he did it with style. The victory was extra special because it proved that American horses could compete with the best international champions. As dust settled on the track that day, Animal Kingdom had earned his owners a whopping $6 million prize and brought serious bragging rights back to America.

title: Racing Into History at 203 MPH
story: Henry Segrave gripped the steering wheel of his sleek Sunbeam race car and prepared to make history on March 29th, 1927. The sandy beaches of Daytona stretched ahead like a natural racetrack, perfect for his daring attempt. When Segrave hit the gas, his car roared forward faster than any human had ever traveled on land before. The speedometer climbed higher and higher until it reached an incredible 203.79 miles per hour! That's faster than most cars can go today on highways. Segrave had just shattered the world land speed record, proving that with enough courage and engineering skill, humans could push the boundaries of what seemed possible.
activity_question: What speed did Henry Segrave reach in his record-breaking run?
bonus_fact: Daytona's hard-packed sand made it perfect for early speed records before racetracks existed.

title: Ice Dancing Champions Without a Country
story: Two Russian ice dancers found themselves in a very strange situation on March 29th, 1992, when they won the World Ice Dance Championship in Oakland, California. Marina Klimova and Sergei Ponomarenko were incredible on the ice, gliding and spinning their way to victory with perfect grace. But here's the weird part - they couldn't compete for Russia because the Soviet Union had just broken apart! Instead, they had to represent something called the CIS (Commonwealth of Independent States), which was basically a temporary team name for athletes from the former Soviet countries. Imagine winning gold but not knowing exactly which flag would be raised for you!
activity_question: What did CIS stand for when Klimova and Ponomarenko won their championship?
follow_up_question: How do you think it would feel to win a world championship but not be sure which country you're representing?

title: Atlanta's Brand New Baseball Home
story: The Atlanta Braves had a big problem in 1997 - they needed a new stadium! Their old one was getting torn down after the Olympics. So on March 29th that year, something exciting happened: the very first baseball game at their shiny new Turner Field. It was just a practice game against the famous New York Yankees, but the Braves wanted to make a good first impression in their new home. And they did! The Braves won 2-0, which was pretty awesome for a team playing in a brand new ballpark. The players got to run around the bases, the fans got to cheer from new seats, and everyone got to see what would become the Braves' home for many years.

It was played in the snow

title: Australia's Cricket Championship Victory
story: Cricket fans around the world held their breath as Australia faced New Zealand in the Cricket World Cup final at Melbourne's famous MCG stadium on March 29th, 2015. The Aussies were playing for their fourth world championship title on home soil, with thousands of cheering fans packed into the massive stadium. Australia's bowlers struck first, with James Faulkner taking three crucial wickets for just 36 runs to slow down New Zealand's batting. Then Australia's batters stepped up big time, smashing the winning runs with 101 balls still left to play - that's like finishing a race way ahead of everyone else!
activity_question: How many Cricket World Cup titles had Australia won after this victory?
bonus_fact: The MCG stadium can hold over 100,000 people - that's like a small city!

title: Ice Dancing Gold from a Vanished Country
story: Two figure skaters made history on March 29th, 1992, but they competed for a country that was disappearing! Natalia Mishkutenok and Artur Dmitriev glided across the ice in Oakland, California, spinning and leaping their way to victory at the Ice Pairs Championship. The twist? They represented the CIS - the Commonwealth of Independent States - which was what remained after the Soviet Union broke apart just months earlier. While their homeland was changing forever, these talented athletes proved that some things, like perfect teamwork on ice, stay beautiful no matter what's happening in the world around them.
activity_question: What did CIS stand for when Mishkutenok and Dmitriev won their championship?
bonus_fact: The CIS only lasted about one year as a competing nation in sports!

title: Queen Victoria's Grand Opening
story: Queen Victoria had a big problem on March 29th, 1871 - she was supposed to open a brand new concert hall in London, but she was still sad about her husband Albert's death ten years earlier. The Royal Albert Hall was actually named after him! When the Queen stood up to give her speech that day, something amazing happened. She got so emotional thinking about Albert that she couldn't finish her words. The crowd understood and cheered anyway, officially opening one of the world's most famous music venues.
activity_question: What happened when Queen Victoria tried to give her opening speech?
activity_choices: She couldn't finish because she got too emotional
She sang a song instead of speaking
She forgot all her words completely
She spoke for three hours straight

title: Baby Astronaut Takes First Breath
story: A future space explorer took his very first breath on March 29th, 1965, when William Oefelein was born in Fort Belvoir, Virginia. Little did anyone know that this tiny baby would grow up to zoom around Earth at 17,500 miles per hour! Years later, Oefelein became a NASA astronaut and blasted off on the Space Shuttle Discovery for mission STS-116. His job was to help build the International Space Station, floating in zero gravity while constructing humanity's home among the stars. From a military base in Virginia to the endless black of space, this March birthday boy proved that dreams really can reach beyond the clouds.
activity_question: What was the name of the space shuttle mission that William Oefelein flew on?
bonus_fact: The International Space Station travels around Earth once every 90 minutes!

title: Cricket's Epic Six-Day Battle Begins
story: Two cricket teams stepped onto the field in Wellington, New Zealand on March 29th, 1946, ready to make history. Australia and New Zealand were about to play their very first official Test cricket match against each other! Test cricket is the longest form of the sport, where games can last up to five days. activity_question: How long can a Test cricket match last?

title: The Longest Spring Training Game Ever
story: Baseball fans got way more than they bargained for when the New York Yankees and Boston Red Sox faced off in spring training on this date back in 1948! What started as a regular practice game turned into an epic marathon that lasted 17 innings. Both teams kept scoring and then getting shut down, over and over again. Players were probably getting pretty tired by the 10th inning, but they kept playing. Finally, after what felt like forever, the game ended in a 2-2 tie. That's one way to get extra batting practice!

title: America's First Male Gymnastics Gold Hero
story: Kurt Thomas flipped into history when he was born in Miami, Florida on this very date in 1956! This future gymnastics superstar would become the first American male ever to win a gold medal at the World Championships. In 1978, he dazzled judges with his floor routine, then came back in 1979 to win gold again on both floor and horizontal bars. But here's the twist - he also became a movie star in an action film called "Gymkata," where he used his incredible gymnastics skills to fight bad guys!

title: Greece's Golden Sprint to Glory
story: A future Olympic champion took her first breath in the small Greek village of Tripotamo on this very day in 1965. activity_question: What Olympic event did Voula Patoulidou win gold in?
bonus_fact: Her victory was Greece's first Olympic track and field gold medal ever!

title: Ohio's Big Work Rule Change
story: Back on this date in 1852, Ohio did something pretty unusual - they made a new law saying kids under 18 and women couldn't work more than 10 hours a day. That might sound like a long time now, but back then, many people worked 12 or even 14 hours every single day! This was one of the first times a state said "Hey, that's too much work for some people." It was a big step toward making work safer and fairer, even though it would take many more years before work rules became truly equal for everyone.

title: Brazil's First Capital Gets Born
story: Portuguese explorers picked the perfect spot along Brazil's coast and founded Salvador da Bahia on March 29th, 1549, making it the very first capital city of this huge South American country! The city's name means "Savior of the Bay," and it sat right next to a beautiful harbor where ships could safely dock. For over 200 years, this colorful city served as Brazil's most important place, welcoming traders, settlers, and adventurers from around the world before the capital eventually moved to Rio de Janeiro.

title: Sweden's American Adventure Begins
story: Peter Minuit had a wild idea that would change history forever on March 29th, 1638! This brave explorer sailed across the dangerous Atlantic Ocean with the very first Swedish families to reach America. They landed on the Delaware Peninsula and founded New Sweden, creating the first Swedish colony in the New World. These tough pioneers brought their own language, customs, and dreams of a better life to a completely unknown land thousands of miles from home.

title: America's First Federal Highway Gets the Green Light
story: Congress made history on March 29th, 1806, when they said "yes" to building America's very first federal highway! The Great National Pike, which most people called the Cumberland Road, would stretch from Maryland all the way to Illinois. Before this amazing road, traveling across the country meant bumping along terrible dirt paths that turned into muddy messes when it rained. This new highway would change everything, making it much easier for families to move west and for goods to travel between states.
activity_question: What was the popular nickname for the Great National Pike?

title: Ravens Rise from the Ashes
story: The Cleveland Browns were packing up and moving to Baltimore, but they needed a brand new name for their fresh start. On March 29th, 1996, team owners announced their choice: the Baltimore Ravens! The name came from Edgar Allan Poe's famous spooky poem "The Raven," since the writer had lived in Baltimore. title: The Cuban Boxing Legend's Birthday
story: March 29th, 1952 marked the birth of a future boxing superstar in Puerto Padre, Cuba. Teofilo Stevenson was born that day and would grow up to become one of the greatest amateur boxers ever. Campbell earned the nickname "The Tyler Rose" and proved he was special by winning the Heisman Trophy in 1977 at the University of Texas. title: Sweden's Surprise American Colony
story: Swedish explorers sailed across the Atlantic Ocean and built Fort Christina on March 29th, 1638, creating Delaware's very first permanent white settlement! These brave Swedish Lutherans chose a spot along the Delaware River that would later become the city of Wilmington. It was pretty bold for Sweden to start a colony so far from home, competing with bigger countries like England and Spain for land in the New World.

bonus_fact: The Swedish colony was called New Sweden and lasted about 17 years.

title: Cricket's Closest Call Ever
story: March 29th, 1982 delivered one of cricket's most nail-biting finishes ever! In India's biggest cricket tournament, the Ranji Trophy, two teams battled it out with incredible skill. Delhi scored 707 runs in their first innings, while Karnataka managed 705 runs. Just two runs separated victory from defeat! Delhi won the championship by the tiniest possible margin, proving that in cricket, every single run counts.

story: A comedian named Jack Benny stepped up to a microphone for the very first time on March 29th, 1932, appearing on Ed Sullivan's New York interview program. Benny would go on to make millions of people laugh with his funny timing and pretend stinginess, becoming so popular that families would gather around their radios just to hear his voice every week.

story: Amy Sedaris burst into the world on March 29th, 1961, in Endicott, New York, destined to become one of America's funniest people. title: The Great Island Giveaway
story: Great Britain did something pretty unusual on May 2nd, 1864 - they gave away a bunch of beautiful islands! The Ionian Islands, including the famous vacation spot of Corfu, were handed back to Greece after being under British control for decades. title: A New Kind of College Opens
story: March 29th, 1928 marked a special day for education in New York City when Yeshiva College received its official charter to operate as a real college. This wasn't just any school - it was the first college in America designed to let Jewish students study both regular subjects like math and science AND their religious traditions at the same time. Before this, students often had to choose one or the other, but Yeshiva College said "Why not both?" The school later grew so big and successful that it became Yeshiva University, proving that mixing different kinds of learning can create something amazing.

It only taught math and science
It was the biggest college in America
It had classes taught by robots

title: Cricket's Ultimate Bowling Masterclass
story: March 29th, 1946 became one of cricket's most lopsided days when Australia demolished New Zealand for just 42 runs in Wellington. Bill O'Reilly and Ernie Toshack bowled like magicians, taking nine wickets between them and leaving the Kiwis stunned. This match also marked the debut of three future Australian legends who would dominate cricket for years to come.
activity_question: How many runs did New Zealand score in their disastrous innings?
bonus_fact: This remains one of the lowest Test cricket scores ever recorded in international play.

title: A Soccer Dream Takes Root
story: Kids in the Dutch town of Drachten had a big dream in 1948 - they wanted their own soccer team! On this day that year, the Drachtster Boys soccer club was officially born. The young players probably had no idea their team would still be kicking goals decades later. Starting a sports club might sound easy, but it takes real teamwork and dedication to make it last.
activity_question: In which country was the Drachtster Boys soccer team formed?
bonus_fact: Drachten is famous for removing all its traffic lights in a safety experiment!

title: Vienna's Biggest Goodbye Ever
story: Vienna had never seen anything like it when Ludwig van Beethoven died on March 26th, 1827. Three days later, 20,000 people crowded the streets for his funeral — that's like filling a huge stadium! Even though Beethoven was completely deaf by then, his music had touched so many hearts that the entire city came to say goodbye to their beloved composer.
activity_question: How many people attended Beethoven's funeral in Vienna?
bonus_fact: Beethoven wrote his most famous symphonies after he went completely deaf.

title: The Sea Captain Who Saved Children
story: Thomas Coram sailed the seas as a ship captain, but his biggest adventure happened on land. Born in Lyme Regis on this very date back in 1668, he would grow up to become something amazing — a hero for children who had nowhere to go. Coram founded the Foundling Hospital, a safe place for kids without families.
activity_question: What did Thomas Coram create to help children?
bonus_fact: The Foundling Hospital was one of the first places in England to care for abandoned babies.

title: The Kora Master's Musical Magic
story: A baby boy named Mory Kanté was born on this day in 1950 in a small village in French Guinea, Africa. He would grow up to become famous around the world for playing the kora, a beautiful harp-like instrument with 21 strings. His biggest hit song was called "Yé ké yé ké," which means "it's all good" in his language!
activity_question: What instrument did Mory Kanté become famous for playing?
bonus_fact: The kora is made from a large gourd and sounds like a guitar and harp combined!
